description:
You are assisting a cloud-migration architect who wants a quick, repeatable spot-check of how well a fresh VM is handling (1) sequential disk writes and (2) basic integer CPU calculations.  
Create a one-off benchmark and save the results in a log file so that an automated report generator can later pick them up.

Required final state of the system
1. Directory  
   • /home/user/benchmarks must exist.  
2. File  
   • /home/user/benchmarks/performance.log must exist, be a plain text file, and be world-readable (mode 0644 or more open).  
3. performance.log MUST contain exactly three non-blank lines in the precise order shown below.  
   • Line-1 starts with the literal string “DISK_WRITE_MB_S: ” (note the single space after the colon) and is immediately followed by the numeric throughput reported by dd, expressed in megabytes per second with at least one decimal place.  
   • Line-2 starts with the literal string “CPU_EVENTS_PER_SEC: ” (single space after the colon) and is immediately followed by the numeric “events per second” value reported by sysbench, written with at least one decimal place.  
   • Line-3 starts with the literal string “TIMESTAMP: ” (single space) followed by the local date-time in the fixed format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (24-hour clock, leading zeros required).  
   Example of a syntactically valid file (numbers will differ on your system):  
   DISK_WRITE_MB_S: 456.8  
   CPU_EVENTS_PER_SEC: 8731.2  
   TIMESTAMP: 2023-11-09 14:32:01  

What you have to do (high-level steps; choose the exact commands and flags yourself)
a. Create the /home/user/benchmarks directory if it doesn’t already exist.  
b. Run a sequential disk-write test that writes exactly 64 MiB of zeros to a temporary file inside that directory, using dd with fsync (so the kernel actually flushes to disk). Capture the final “MB/s” figure that dd prints.  
c. Run a small CPU benchmark consisting of calculating prime numbers up to 10 000 using sysbench (or install it first if it is missing). Capture the “events per second” figure from sysbench’s summary.  
d. Delete the temporary 64 MiB file so it doesn’t waste disk space.  
e. Compose /home/user/benchmarks/performance.log exactly as described above, inserting the two numeric results you captured and the current timestamp.  
f. Make sure the file’s permissions allow later read-only access by other non-privileged users.

Important notes & constraints for automated grading
• The three lines in performance.log must end with a newline (ASCII 0x0A).  
• Do NOT include any additional lines, comments, units, or blank lines.  
• Both numeric values must be parsable as POSIX floating-point numbers (digits, optional decimal point, optional fractional part, no thousands separators).  
• The timestamp must match the strict format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS and reflect the system’s current local time when the log file is written.  
• The log file must be located exactly at /home/user/benchmarks/performance.log and nowhere else.  

That’s all—when the file exists with the correct contents and permissions, the benchmark task is considered complete.
truth:
Directory that must be present:
  /home/user/benchmarks

File that must be created:
  /home/user/benchmarks/performance.log

Regular-expression based verification (each ^ and $ applies to the whole line):
  Line 1: ^DISK_WRITE_MB_S: [0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$
  Line 2: ^CPU_EVENTS_PER_SEC: [0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$
  Line 3: ^TIMESTAMP: [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}$

Total number of lines in the file must be exactly 3.

Permissions for /home/user/benchmarks/performance.log must be <= 0644 (i.e., readable by others but not world-writable).

The automated test will fail if:
  • The file is not found at the exact path.
  • The file has more or fewer than 3 lines.
  • Any of the three lines do not match the regexes above.
  • Any of the mandatory literal prefixes differ in spelling or case.
  • File permissions are more permissive than 0666.
